originalTitle: There is minimal downside to switching to open models
content:
There is minimal downside to switching to open models

Andrew Marble marble.onl andrew@willows.ai June 21, 2026

There was a time not too long ago when using Linux entailed some professional risk 1 . First there was compatibility: you may not have been able to render a Word document or PowerPoint correctly, and you might have had to trust Open Office’s export capability to render docs the way you wanted. There might have been specialty file formats you couldn’t easily view and so couldn’t collaborate. And second, the software ecosystem was just worse generally. There were lots of half-build open-source projects trying to achieve the functionality of mainstream software, but they always had rough edges. I, embarrassingly, stayed on Windows until I left academia over Matlab.

Nowadays I think this issue has largely disappeared. Most productivity software has a web-app, Linux is more mature, open-source software is better. I’m sure that there are all sorts of application specific software (CAD?) that still require a Windows machine, but the gap is much narrower and Linux + open source generally aren’t the “sacrifice” they once were generally.

There remains a clear penalty for being an open 2 LLM user. Every leaderboard consistently gets topped by proprietary models served over API. Today on June 21, 2026, Claude and GPT are at the top of the Artificial Analysis intelligence leaderboard. That’s from the performance side. The compatibility side is worse too. Claude code just works, and more generally, the big two provide nice APIs that make them easy to use, and, even if it’s a low bar, are “trustworthy” in the sense that we’ve largely all agreed we don’t mind sending them our LLM queries and trust them to handle them appropriately.

Open models are served via various means, some by the companies that released them and some by third parties like OpenRouter. Unfortunately, both of these routes are dodgier in terms of privacy and data sharing, and I would not feel the same comfort sending API calls containing client or confidential data to them 3 .

The other option or course is to run them yourself. This solves the privacy issue but is at least two of expensive, complicated, and comparatively slow.

Up until recently, open models had mostly been a hobby for me. I’ve tinkered with them since the original Llama leak, and occasionally used them when I has a niche use case, but for most professional work, I stuck with the Big 2. This appears to be changing, with Claude’s ID verification rollout 4 . It was inevitable that things would get worse for users, and the writing was on the wall anyway recently with all the new “safeguards” on recent models and the whole Mythos thing. I’m not going to spend time talking about why I’m not going to indulge ID verification (or the LARPing that surrounds it) but what is immediately concerning is what kind of professional penalty it will incur to stop using the top models.

I’m hoping it’s going to be minimal. I’m already set up to run a range of open models either locally or in the cloud, there are good coding harnesses for open models, and most importantly the open models are now very close to the leaders and typically trail only by a few months. This doesn’t feel like 2008 Linux vs Windows, it’s much closer. I expect productivity will take a short-term hit, but don’t think it’s a deal breaker the way switching from Matlab to GNU Octave would have been when I was doing research.

I’m assuming a technical job that includes general purpose work that requires productivity software like MS Office etc. ↩︎

I use “open” here to mean the weights are available, I have written extensively on why I don’t consider this automatically open source, but I’m using “open” as shorthand. And unlike when I addressed this previously, the current leading open models generally are MIT licensed which I do consider open source, though many don’t. ↩︎

I won’t dwell on this, happy to be corrected, but in my experience under normal circumstances nobody balks if you tell them you’re using OpenAI or Anthropic. If you’re sending requests to Deepseek or OpenRouter etc. there are likely to be more concerns, regardless of the underlying truth. ↩︎

https://support.claude.com/en/articles/14328960-identity-verification-on-claude ↩︎
chineseTitle: 切换到开放模型的负面影响很小
chineseContent:
切换到开放模型的负面影响很小

安德鲁·大理石 Marble.onl andrew@willows.ai 2026 年 6 月 21 日

不久前，使用 Linux 曾一度带来一些职业风险 1 。首先是兼容性：您可能无法正确呈现 Word 文档或 PowerPoint，并且您可能必须信任 Open Office 的导出功能才能按照您想要的方式呈现文档。可能存在您无法轻松查看的特殊文件格式，因此无法进行协作。其次，软件生态系统总体上更糟糕。有很多半构建的开源项目试图实现主流软件的功能，但它们总是有粗糙的边缘。令人尴尬的是，我一直使用 Windows，直到我离开学术界转向 Matlab。

如今我认为这个问题已经基本上消失了。大多数生产力软件都有网络应用程序，Linux 更成熟，开源软件更好。我确信有各种特定应用软件（CAD？）仍然需要 Windows 机器，但差距要小得多，而且 Linux + 开源通常不会像以前那样“牺牲”。

作为一名开放的 2 LLM 用户仍然存在明显的惩罚。每个排行榜始终由通过 API 提供服务的专有模型名列前茅。 2026 年 6 月 21 日的今天，Claude 和 GPT 名列人工智能分析排行榜榜首。这是从性能方面来说的。兼容性方面也更差。 Claude 的代码可以正常工作，更一般地说，这两大公司提供了很好的 API，使它们易于使用，而且，即使门槛很低，也是“值得信赖的”，因为我们基本上都同意我们不介意向他们发送我们的 LLM 查询，并相信他们能够正确处理它们。

开放模型通过多种方式提供服务，有些是由发布它们的公司提供的，有些是由 OpenRouter 等第三方提供的。不幸的是，这两种途径在隐私和数据共享方面都比较危险，而且我不会同样放心地向他们发送包含客户端或机密数据的 API 调用 3 。

另一种选择或课程是您自己运行它们。这解决了隐私问题，但至少存在以下两个问题：昂贵、复杂和相对缓慢。

直到最近，开放模型对我来说主要还是一种爱好。自从最初的 Llama 泄露以来，我就一直在修改它们，当我有一个利基用例时，我偶尔会使用它们，但对于大多数专业工作，我坚持使用 Big 2。随着 Claude 的 ID 验证推出 4，这种情况似乎正在改变。对于用户来说，事情会变得更糟是不可避免的，而且最近的情况已经不妙了，因为最近的模型和整个神话的事情都有新的“保障措施”。我不会花时间谈论为什么我不会沉迷于身份验证（或围绕它的 LARPing），但立即令人担忧的是停止使用顶级模型将招致什么样的专业惩罚。

我希望它会是最小的。我已经准备好在本地或云端运行一系列开放模型，开放模型有很好的编码工具，最重要的是，开放模型现在非常接近领先者，通常只落后几个月。这感觉不像 2008 年的 Linux 与 Windows，而是更接近。我预计生产力会受到短期打击，但不要认为这会像我做研究时从 Matlab 切换到 GNU Octave 那样造成交易破坏。

我承担的技术工作包括通用工作，需要 MS Office 等生产力软件。↩︎

我在这里使用“开放”表示权重可用，我已经写了很多关于为什么我不认为它自动开源的文章，但我使用“开放”作为简写。与我之前解决这个问题时不同的是，当前领先的开放模型通常是 MIT 许可的，我确实认为这是开源的，尽管很多模型并不这样做。 ↩︎

我不会详细讨论这一点，很高兴得到纠正，但根据我的经验，在正常情况下，如果你告诉他们你正在使用 OpenAI 或 Anthropic，没有人会犹豫。如果您向 Deepseek 或 OpenRouter 等发送请求，无论底层事实如何，都可能会出现更多问题。 ↩︎

https://support.claude.com/en/articles/14328960-identity-verification-on-claude ↩︎
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于 AI 与科技趋势信号，适合用于观察工具选型、产品机会、开发者关注点和技术路线变化。
